nativeRange.habitat: Captive-bred hybrid; no wild population. Developed in Taiwan circa 1986 from Central American cichlid parentage.

decorPreferences: tall decorations to break line of sight

diet.notes: The Blood Parrot's uniquely shaped, beak-like mouth limits what it can take in, so appropriately sized floating mini pellets or sinking cichlid pellets tend to work best. Supplement the base diet two to three times weekly with protein-rich foods such as bloodworms and brine shrimp. Color-enhancing formulas can help maintain the fish's vibrant coloration.

imageCandidates.notes: Retro Lenses; 3225x2141. Two tack-sharp, vividly orange Blood Parrots in a planted tank, rounded bodies and beak-like mouths clearly shown; best focus and color of the pool.
Fanghong; 2560x1920. Sharp pair showing the orange-and-white coloration variant, rounded body and beak mouth; adds color variety. Minor tank-glass reflection.
Love Krittaya; 2272x1704. Large group of vivid orange-red Blood Parrots, illustrating how the shoaling hybrid is kept in numbers. Slight softness and top glare but many individuals crisp.

summary: The Blood Parrot Cichlid is a man-made hybrid developed in Taiwan around 1986, recognized by its rounded body and vivid orange-red coloration. Its small, beak-like mouth limits what it can bite, making it less aggressive in practice than its semi-aggressive temperament rating suggests. Groups of three or more are recommended.
careNotes:
The Blood Parrot Cichlid has no formal scientific species name because it is a hybrid — the most widely accepted parentage is Amphilophus citrinellus × Paraneetroplus synspilus, though the cross is sometimes cited as A. citrinellus × Vieja melanurus. The hybrid was developed in Taiwan around 1986 and has no wild population.

When keeping multiple Blood Parrots, avoid pairing just two fish, as one tends to dominate the other. Groups of three or more, or a single specimen kept alone, are generally preferred.

A conservative water chemistry range of pH 6.5–7.4 is widely recommended for this hybrid, as the fish's exact tolerances are less well-documented than those of full species.

The distinctive beak-like mouth is a defining trait of the hybrid — it limits the fish's ability to bite and compete for food, and also restricts it to appropriately sized pellets and softer foods. Juveniles may display black markings that typically fade as they mature. Dyed or color-injected specimens, sometimes sold as 'painted parrots', are not recommended, as the dyeing process is stressful and harmful to the fish.

Heart-shaped 'Jellybean Parrot' variants are surgically altered and are also not recommended from an animal welfare standpoint. Natural color variants include orange and red (the most common), yellow, and occasionally white (sold as platinum or snow white). Trade variants include King Kong Parrot, Polar Blue Parrot, and Kirin or Flowerhorn Parrot hybrids.
breedingNotes: Male Blood Parrot Cichlids are typically sterile as a result of the fish's hybrid origins. Females will lay eggs on flat surfaces, and both parents generally guard the clutch, but the eggs rarely develop. Reliable captive breeding has not been achieved by hobbyists. For those who attempt spawning, a water temperature of 24–27°C and a pH of 6.5–7.4 are generally advised.

additional.name: FishKeeping World
additional.notes: Used as secondary cross-check source. Practical Fishkeeping (originally designated secondary) returned HTTP 403 and was inaccessible. SeriouslyFish has no profile for this hybrid species. pH range 6.5-7.4 from this source used as conservative figure vs primary's 6.5-8.0. Adult size min of 15 cm from this source; max of 20 cm consistent with primary. Lifespan 10-15 years from this source (primary did not specify).

fish.breedingNotes: Males are typically sterile. Females can lay eggs and display biparental guarding behavior, but eggs rarely develop. Hobbyists have not been able to reliably produce Blood Parrot offspring.
